title: Receiving Radioactive Material
narrationScript: A replacement source arriving at the shop is the shipping lesson run in reverse, and 10 CFR 20.1906 writes the script. Arrange in advance to take delivery when the carrier offers the package, or to collect it expeditiously when the terminal calls. Once it is in hand, the clock runs: monitor as soon as practicable, never later than three hours after receipt during working hours, or three hours into the next working day for an after-hours arrival. Survey radiation levels on any labeled package above a Type A quantity — a working source always is. Wipe for removable contamination unless the contents are special form or gas, and a crushed, wet, or damaged package gets both checks, no exceptions. The limits come from the transport rules: two hundred millirem per hour at the surface, a transport index of ten, and four becquerels per square centimeter of removable beta-gamma activity. Exceed either one and you immediately notify the final delivery carrier and telephone the NRC Operations Center. Then verify the contents against the papers, record every reading, and secure the source.

sections.heading: The Last Mile Is Yours
sections.body:
The previous lesson ended with a placarded truck rolling toward its destination. This lesson is the other end of that trip: the package is arriving at *your* facility, and **10 CFR 20.1906 — procedures for receiving and opening packages** — scripts everything that happens next. For a radiography licensee this is routine work with high stakes: replacement sources arrive several times a year as the old ones decay away, traveling by common carrier inside a source changer that is itself an NRC-certified Type B package.

The rule starts before the truck does. Under 20.1906(a) the licensee must **make arrangements in advance** to receive the package — either:

- **take delivery when the carrier offers it** at your facility, or
- **receive notification when the package reaches the carrier's terminal and take possession of it expeditiously.**

"Expeditiously" is the word that matters. A Yellow-III package sitting on a freight dock is a radiation source nobody is watching: dock workers stack boxes against it, no one there knows what a transport index means, and the security obligations that follow the source are being met by no one. The licensee — not the carrier — owns that risk once the package stops moving, so radiography companies designate and train who receives sources and keep the carrier's terminal phone numbers current.

Receiving is also your independent check on everyone upstream. The shipper certified the package; the carrier hauled it. The receipt survey is the moment the system verifies — with a calibrated instrument, not a signature — that the source actually arrived shielded, contained, and intact.

sections.heading: The Three-Hour Clock and the Monitoring Matrix
sections.body:
Once the package is received, 20.1906(c) starts a clock: perform the required monitoring **as soon as practicable**, and in no case later than —

- **3 hours after receipt**, if the package arrives during your normal working hours, or
- **3 hours from the beginning of the next working day**, if it arrives after working hours.

What you must monitor depends on the contents. 20.1906(b) builds a small matrix, and a radiographer should be able to run it from memory:

- **External radiation levels** — required for any *labeled* package (one bearing a RADIOACTIVE WHITE-I, YELLOW-II, or YELLOW-III label) unless its contents are at or below the **Type A quantity limits**. A working radiography source obliterates that threshold — the A1 value for Ir-192 is 1 TBq (27 Ci) and new sources commonly arrive at 100 Ci or more — so for source shipments the radiation survey is always required.
- **Removable contamination wipe** — required for any labeled package **unless the contents are only special form material or gas**. A sealed source is special form by design, so an intact source-changer shipment is excused from the wipe by rule — though many license procedures conservatively wipe every package anyway, and your written procedures govern.
- **Evidence of degradation cancels every exemption.** Any package *known to contain radioactive material* that arrives **crushed, wet, or damaged** gets **both** the radiation survey and the contamination wipe — labeled or not, special form or not.

One more provision was written for radiography crews specifically. Under **20.1906(f)**, a licensee transferring special form sources **in its own vehicles to and from a work site** is exempt from the contamination-monitoring requirement — but **not** from the radiation-level monitoring, because that survey is what proves *the source is still properly lodged in its shield*. The survey of the camera coming off your own truck at the end of a job is not just good practice; it is this regulation working.

sections.caption: The 10 CFR 20.1906 receiving map: arrange to receive the package, start the three-hour monitoring clock on receipt, then run the monitoring matrix — radiation levels above Type A quantities, contamination wipes unless the contents are special form or gas, and both checks on any crushed, wet, or damaged package.

sections.heading: Running the Receipt Survey
sections.body:
Treat the receipt survey as a fixed sequence, performed by a trained individual the RSO has designated, using a **calibrated, operable instrument** (10 CFR 20.1501(b)).

**Look before you touch.** Walk around the package: crush damage, punctures, wet spots or staining, scorch marks, a broken security seal. Confirm the markings and labels match the shipping papers — proper shipping name and UN number (UN2916 for a Type B(U) shipment), the package certificate ID, the label category, and the transport index. Damage observed here switches you to the monitor-everything branch and a slower, more deliberate pace.

**Survey radiation levels.** Measure the maximum reading **at the package surface**, then the maximum **at one meter**. The pass-fail line comes from the transport standards of 10 CFR 71.47: for a package in non-exclusive use, **no more than 200 mrem/h (2 mSv/h) at any point on the surface and a transport index of 10**. The one-meter reading also cross-checks the label: a measured 2.1 mrem/h against a labeled TI of 2.1 says the shipment arrived as certified, while a measured 6 against a labeled 2.1 says something has moved inside that crate even though no absolute limit is broken — stop and get the RSO before going further.

**Wipe for removable contamination.** Wipe an area of about **300 cm²** of the external surface with moderate pressure using a dry smear or filter pad, then count it with an appropriate instrument **in a low-background area away from the package** — a smear counted beside a Yellow-III crate measures the crate, not the smear. Unless your program has determined the actual collection efficiency, assume the wipe picked up **10 percent** of what was on the surface (49 CFR 173.443(a)). The limits, averaged over the area wiped: **4 Bq/cm² (10⁻⁴ µCi/cm², about 220 dpm/cm²) for beta-gamma and low-toxicity alpha emitters**, and 0.4 Bq/cm² for all other alpha emitters. Removable contamination on a sealed-source package is never normal — it means the capsule, the changer, or some upstream handling has failed, and it gets treated that way.

sections.caption: The receipt survey on a source-changer crate: inspect for damage, survey the maximum surface and one-meter readings against the 10 CFR 71.47 limits and the labeled TI, wipe 300 cm² for removable contamination, and verify the contents against the shipping papers before anything is opened or stored.

sections.title: Over a limit? Two immediate notifications
sections.body: If receipt monitoring finds **removable contamination above the 10 CFR 71.87(i) limits** or **external radiation levels above the 10 CFR 71.47 limits**, 20.1906(d) requires the licensee to **immediately notify the final delivery carrier and, by telephone, the NRC Operations Center** (301-816-5100, staffed 24 hours); an Agreement State licensee makes the equivalent call to its state radiation-control program. While the RSO works the phones, you work the floor: move people back, isolate the package behind a boundary set by your survey readings, and keep it under surveillance. Do not open it, do not re-ship it, and do not try to "fix" it — that package is now evidence as well as a hazard.

sections.heading: Verify, Document, Secure
sections.body:
**Verify the contents against the paperwork.** The packing list and shipping papers say what should be inside: radionuclide, activity and its assay date, source model and capsule serial number, the special form certificate, and the sealed-source data sheet the supplier ships with every source. Check them against your purchase order and your license — every license carries **possession limits**, and receipt is where you confirm the new source, plus everything already in the vault, still fits inside them. A mismatched serial number or activity is resolved with the supplier before the source enters service, never after.

**Document the receipt.** The survey record carries the date and time of receipt and of monitoring, package identification, the instrument model, serial number, and calibration due date, the maximum surface and one-meter readings, the wipe result, and the signature of the person who did the work. Survey records are retained for **3 years** (10 CFR 20.2103); the receipt itself joins the material-accountability records kept for **as long as you possess the source plus 3 years after its transfer or disposal** (10 CFR 30.51). The new source also enters the **quarterly physical inventory** (10 CFR 34.29) and begins its life in the **utilization log** (10 CFR 34.71).

**Secure it immediately.** A receiving dock is an unrestricted area, and the two-state rule from the storage lesson applies the moment the carrier drives away: constant surveillance or locked away, nothing in between (10 CFR 20.1801–20.1802). A new 100 Ci Ir-192 source is a **category 2 quantity**, so Part 37 security — the security zone, trustworthiness-and-reliability access, detection and assessment — wraps around it from the dock to the vault.

**Opening comes later, by procedure.** 20.1906(e) requires **written procedures for safely opening packages**, followed with due consideration for the special instructions that apply to the package type. For a source changer that means the manufacturer's instructions, trained personnel, and an authorized source exchange — never a curious forklift driver with a pry bar.

And if an expected source never arrives? Call the shipper and start the trace at once. A shipment determined to be **lost or missing** must be reported by the shipping licensee to the NRC Operations Center — within **1 hour for category 1** quantities and **4 hours for category 2** (10 CFR 37.81) — and your prompt call to the shipper is what starts that clock.

sections.caption: The numbers that govern receipt of radioactive material — commit these to memory.
rows: Monitoring deadline — after receipt during normal working hours, or from the beginning of the next working day for after-hours arrivals
Type A quantity (A1 for Ir-192: 1 TBq / 27 Ci)
Labeled packages above it require a radiation-level survey on receipt — every working source shipment qualifies
Special form or gas contents only
Excuses the contamination wipe on an intact labeled package — crushed, wet, or damaged packages get both checks regardless
Maximum external radiation for a non-exclusive-use package — receipt readings above these trigger notifications
Removable beta-gamma contamination limit (other alpha emitters: 0.4 Bq/cm²); assume 10% wipe pickup unless determined
Notify the final delivery carrier and, by telephone, the NRC Operations Center when either limit is exceeded
Retention of receipt survey records (material receipt records: for the life of possession plus 3 years after transfer or disposal)

figures.title: Receiving a radioactive package — the 10 CFR 20.1906 decision map
The receipt survey, step by step

interactive.title: Package Receipt Survey Walkthrough
interactive.intro: Tuesday, 1:30 p.m. A freight carrier's truck pulls up to your shop with a source changer holding the company's new 92-curie (3.4 TBq) Ir-192 source — the papers read UN2916, RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III, TI 2.1. The RSO has designated you to receive it. Walk the package in the way 10 CFR 20.1906 demands.

steps.prompt: The driver wheels the crate onto your dock and holds out the clipboard. What does accepting this package commit you to?
options: Sign, stack it with the morning's other freight, and let the source-exchange crew deal with it next week
Sign for it, then monitor it as soon as practicable — and no later than 4:30 p.m., three hours after receipt during working hours
Refuse delivery until the RSO is physically present, since only the RSO may receive radioactive material
Sign, then survey it within 24 hours — the standard deadline for radioactive receipts
steps.feedback: 20.1906(a) obligates the licensee to arrange to take delivery when the carrier offers it, and 20.1906(c) starts the clock at receipt: monitoring as soon as practicable, no later than 3 hours during working hours (after-hours arrivals get 3 hours from the start of the next working day — never 24). Any trained individual the RSO designates may perform it; an unmonitored crate parked in the freight pile is exactly what the rule exists to prevent.

steps.prompt: Walking the crate before touching anything, you find one corner crushed inward and a dried water stain down a side panel. The contents are a special form sealed source. What monitoring does 20.1906(b) now require?
options: Radiation levels only — special form contents excuse the contamination wipe
A contamination wipe only — the changer's shielding takes care of radiation
Both a radiation-level survey and a removable-contamination wipe — evidence of degradation cancels every exemption
None — Type B packages are accident-tested, so external checks are optional
steps.feedback: The special-form exemption applies only to an intact package. A package known to contain radioactive material that arrives crushed, wet, or damaged must be monitored for BOTH radiation levels and contamination — the damage is exactly the evidence that the package may no longer be doing its job. Type B certification proves the design was tested, not that this crate survived this trip.

steps.prompt: Your 300 cm² wipe counts out at 0.2 Bq/cm² — well under the limit. But the survey meter finds 230 mrem/h at one point on the crate surface. What happens now?
options: Log the reading in the receipt file — the wipe passed, so the package is acceptable
Have the driver take the crate back; an over-limit package is the shipper's problem
Open the changer to check whether the shield shifted in transit
Move people back and isolate the crate, then immediately notify the final delivery carrier and, by telephone, the NRC Operations Center — 230 exceeds the 200 mrem/h surface limit of 10 CFR 71.47
steps.feedback: 20.1906(d) is unambiguous: external radiation beyond the 71.47 limits means immediate notification of the final delivery carrier and a telephone call to the NRC Operations Center (Agreement State licensees call their state program). A passing wipe does not offset a failing radiation survey, sending the crate back puts an over-limit package on the public highway, and opening a package that is already misbehaving is how a receipt problem becomes an exposure.

steps.prompt: The RSO has made the calls, and guidance is coming. Until it arrives, the package —
options: goes into the vault with the other sources so at least it is secured
stays in an isolated, posted area with boundaries set by your survey readings, under surveillance, unopened and unmoved — while you record readings, times, the instrument used, and everyone involved
is transferred into a spare crate so the dock can reopen for normal freight
can be put to work — the source itself is fine even if the packaging is not
steps.feedback: Distance, isolation, and a measured boundary protect people; surveillance and documentation protect the investigation. Wheeling a 230 mrem/h crate into the vault gives your own staff an unnecessary dose en route, repackaging destroys the evidence the NRC and carrier will need, and nothing about a failed package survey clears the source for use. Hold the line, write everything down, and wait for the people whose job the next step is.

checkQuestions.question: A labeled package of radioactive material is delivered to your licensed facility at 9:40 p.m., after working hours. Under 10 CFR 20.1906(c), the required receipt monitoring must be performed:
options: within 3 hours of the package's arrival, even if staff must be called in
within 24 hours of arrival
as soon as practicable, and no later than 3 hours from the beginning of the next working day
before the package is placed in storage, with no fixed time limit
checkQuestions.explanation: 20.1906(c) sets two clocks: packages received during normal working hours are monitored as soon as practicable and within 3 hours of receipt; packages received after hours are monitored within 3 hours from the beginning of the next working day. There is no 24-hour allowance, and "whenever it gets stored" is not a deadline at all.

checkQuestions.question: Which received package may skip the removable-contamination wipe under 10 CFR 20.1906(b)?
options: An undamaged labeled package whose only contents are special form sealed sources (or gas)
Any package bearing a RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III label
A package that arrives crushed, provided its contents are special form
Any package that traveled in exclusive-use transport
checkQuestions.explanation: The wipe is excused only when the contents are exclusively special form material or gas — the forms that cannot produce loose surface contamination from an intact containment. The exemption dies the moment there is evidence of degradation: a crushed, wet, or damaged package is monitored for both contamination and radiation levels regardless of contents, and the label category or transport mode never excuses monitoring.

checkQuestions.question: A receipt wipe over 300 cm² of a package surface is counted for removable beta-gamma contamination. The limit that, if exceeded, triggers the notifications of 10 CFR 20.1906(d) is:
options: 0.4 Bq/cm² averaged over the area wiped
200 mrem/h at the package surface
40 Bq/cm² at any single point
4 Bq/cm² (about 220 dpm/cm²) averaged over the area wiped
checkQuestions.explanation: The removable-contamination limit of 10 CFR 71.87(i) and 49 CFR 173.443 for beta-gamma and low-toxicity alpha emitters is 4 Bq/cm² (10⁻⁴ µCi/cm², about 220 dpm/cm²) averaged over the 300 cm² wiped; 0.4 Bq/cm² is the tighter limit for other alpha emitters. The 200 mrem/h figure is the surface radiation-level limit of 71.47 — a different measurement answering a different question.

checkQuestions.question: Your receipt survey finds external radiation levels on a package exceeding the 10 CFR 71.47 limits. 10 CFR 20.1906(d) requires the licensee to:
options: submit a written report to the NRC within 30 days
immediately notify the final delivery carrier and, by telephone, the NRC Operations Center
notify the shipper within 24 hours and quarantine the package pending instructions
return the package to the carrier for redelivery to the shipper
checkQuestions.explanation: Over-limit contamination or radiation levels on a received package demand immediate notification of two parties: the final delivery carrier and, by telephone, the NRC Operations Center (Agreement State licensees notify their state program). A 30-day letter is far too slow for a package that may have been exposing handlers along its whole route, and shipping the package anywhere puts it right back into public commerce.

checkQuestions.question: A crew returns to the shop in a company truck with the licensee's own exposure device after a field job. Under 10 CFR 20.1906(f), this transfer of a special form source in a licensee-operated vehicle is:
options: exempt from all receipt monitoring requirements
subject to the full contamination-wipe protocol within 3 hours
exempt only if the round trip was completed in a single working day
exempt from contamination monitoring — but the radiation-level check is still required, because it is what proves the source is properly lodged in its shield
checkQuestions.explanation: 20.1906(f) exempts special form sources moved in licensee-owned or licensee-operated vehicles to and from a work site from the contamination-monitoring requirement only. The radiation-level monitoring survives precisely because it confirms the source is still seated in its shielded position — the same verification logic behind every post-exposure and pre-storage survey a radiographer performs.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §11.4 (receiving instructions — radioactive material)
10 CFR 20.1906 — Procedures for receiving and opening packages (with §20.1501(b) calibrated instruments and §20.2103 survey records)
10 CFR Part 71 — Packaging and Transportation of Radioactive Material (§71.47 external radiation standards; §71.87(i) removable contamination limits)
49 CFR 173.435 and 173.443 — A1/A2 quantity values and contamination control (wipe method and removable-contamination limits)
10 CFR Part 30 (§30.51 records of receipt and transfer); 10 CFR Part 34 (§§34.29, 34.71 inventory and utilization logs); 10 CFR Part 37, Subpart D (§37.81 lost or missing shipments)
